nativeRange.habitat: The Saddleback Hogfish is found on reefs across the Indo-West Pacific, ranging from shallow coastal areas to deep outer reef slopes at depths of 3 to 160 meters. Juveniles typically occupy shallower zones, while adults favor deeper reef faces where benthic invertebrates are abundant.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on benthic hard-shelled invertebrates including snails, mussels, worms, crustaceans, and small fish. Hunts by overturning rubble and blowing water across sandy substrate to flush prey. Generally accepts frozen meaty foods such as krill, mysis, chopped clam, shrimp, and squid in captivity.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 3736x2491. Tack-sharp in-situ subadult, whole fish in frame, vivid; clearly shows the diagnostic dark oval saddle at the caudal peduncle, fine-striped body, maroon/white facial bars and yellow posterior.

Totti; 3730x2798. Sharp, colorful adult in an aquarium showing the classic brownish-purple upper body, yellow lateral lines and the black saddle blotch; minor caveat is visible tank equipment in the background.
Diego Delso; 2563x1709. Sharp in-situ juvenile showing the bright yellow-and-black juvenile phase described in the care notes; adds life-stage variety.

summary: The Saddleback Hogfish is a large Indo-Pacific wrasse named for the dark oval saddle marking near its tail. Juveniles are peaceful and act as parasite cleaners for other fish, while adults grow into active predators of reef invertebrates, requiring a large, fish-only system.
careNotes:
The Saddleback Hogfish (also widely traded as Tarry Hogfish or Hawaiian Hogfish) is generally considered an intermediate-level species. Juveniles are peaceful and have been observed picking parasites off tankmates, while adults grow into substantial predators that tend to view most reef invertebrates as food. Once mature, they have been known to consume ornamental shrimp, small crabs, snails, clams, tubeworms, and very small fish, and are generally regarded as not reef-safe once fully grown despite being benign in their juvenile phase.

This species undergoes a dramatic color change with age. Juveniles display bright yellow and black markings that gradually migrate toward the rear of the body and fade with maturity, until the adult shows the characteristic dark oval saddle near the caudal peduncle (the narrow section of the body just before the tail fin) — a marking that may itself become less distinct in large terminal males. The species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a group eventually transitioning to male; this change has been reported at roughly 38 to 40 cm body length.

Given an adult size approaching 55 cm and active, roving hunting habits, the Saddleback Hogfish is suited only to large aquariums. Ample rockwork with caves and overhangs, along with a sandy substrate it can sift through, are important parts of a well-planned setup for this fish.
breedingNotes: The Saddleback Hogfish is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as females, with the dominant fish in a social group eventually transitioning to male — a change reported at roughly 38 to 40 cm body length. Wild spawning is oviparous (egg-laying), with distinct pair formation during reproduction. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Hogfish collection page lists Saddleback Hogfish with care summary: 22 in adult, 150 gal minimum, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive, reef-unsafe at adulthood, carnivore, moderate care level.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Wild spawning is oviparous with distinct pair formation. The species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, with all individuals beginning life as females and the dominant fish in a group later transitioning to male.
